questions.q: By what evocative name was the Cellular Jail commonly known?
questions.explanation: The Cellular Jail was popularly called Kala Pani, meaning 'black water', referring to the dreaded sea voyage and isolation it represented.

questions.q: On which group of islands was the Cellular Jail built?
questions.explanation: The Cellular Jail was constructed by the British on the Andaman Islands, far out in the Bay of Bengal.

questions.q: What feature of the jail's design gave it the name 'Cellular'?
options: Prisoners were kept in individual solitary cells
It was made entirely of glass
questions.explanation: The jail was named 'Cellular' because each prisoner was confined alone in an individual cell, enforcing solitary confinement.

questions.q: Which colonial power built and operated the Cellular Jail?
questions.explanation: The Cellular Jail was built and run by the British to imprison those who resisted colonial rule.

questions.q: What kind of prisoners was the Cellular Jail mainly used to hold?
questions.explanation: The Cellular Jail was used chiefly to imprison political prisoners and freedom fighters of the Indian independence movement.

questions.q: Which body of water surrounds the Andaman Islands where the Cellular Jail stands?
questions.explanation: The Andaman Islands, and thus the Cellular Jail, lie in the Bay of Bengal.

questions.q: The Cellular Jail today serves primarily as what?
questions.explanation: The Cellular Jail has been preserved as a national memorial honouring the freedom fighters who suffered there.

questions.q: Why was sending a prisoner to the Andamans considered an especially severe punishment?
options: It meant complete isolation across the sea from the mainland
questions.explanation: Transportation to the remote Andamans cut prisoners off entirely from family and homeland across the sea, making it a feared punishment.

questions.q: The harsh labour and solitary confinement at the Cellular Jail were meant to do what?
options: Break the spirit of those resisting colonial rule
questions.explanation: The brutal regime of solitary confinement and forced labour was designed to break the will of nationalist prisoners.

questions.q: In which present-day Indian Union Territory is the Cellular Jail located?
questions.explanation: The Cellular Jail stands in Port Blair, the capital of the Andaman and Nicobar Islands Union Territory.

questions.q: In which town on the Andaman Islands is the Cellular Jail situated?
questions.explanation: The Cellular Jail is located in Port Blair, the principal town and capital of the Andaman Islands.

questions.q: The Cellular Jail's construction was largely completed in which period?
questions.explanation: Construction of the Cellular Jail ran from 1896 and was completed around 1906.

questions.q: What was the architectural layout of the Cellular Jail's wings?
options: Radiating spokes from a central tower
questions.explanation: The jail's wings radiated like the spokes of a wheel from a central watchtower, allowing guards to observe all wings.

questions.q: Which prominent revolutionary, author of a history of the 1857 revolt, was transported to the Cellular Jail?
questions.explanation: Vinayak Damodar Savarkar was sentenced to transportation and imprisoned in the Cellular Jail's solitary cells.

questions.q: How many cells did the completed Cellular Jail originally contain?
questions.explanation: The Cellular Jail was built with 698 individual cells across its seven radiating wings.

questions.q: Why were the cells designed so that prisoners in adjacent cells could not face one another?
options: To prevent communication and enforce isolation
questions.explanation: The cells were deliberately arranged so the front of one cell faced the back of another, preventing prisoners from communicating.

questions.q: A notorious form of forced labour at the jail required prisoners to extract oil using what device?
questions.explanation: Prisoners were forced to grind oil on the kolhu, an arduous mill that often demanded an impossible daily quota of oil.

questions.q: A major hunger strike by political prisoners at the Cellular Jail took place in which year?
questions.explanation: In 1933 political prisoners launched a prolonged hunger strike protesting the inhuman conditions, during which several died.

questions.q: Many prisoners associated with which 1915 revolutionary conspiracy were sent to the Cellular Jail?
questions.explanation: Numerous Ghadar revolutionaries convicted in conspiracy cases were transported to the Cellular Jail.

questions.q: The settlement at the Andamans for transported convicts was first established after which event?
questions.explanation: The British established a penal settlement in the Andamans after the Revolt of 1857 to deport rebels, predating the Cellular Jail itself.

questions.q: Savarkar's elder brother, Ganesh Damodar Savarkar, shared what experience with him at the jail?
options: Both brothers were imprisoned in the Cellular Jail
questions.explanation: Both Vinayak and his brother Ganesh (Babarao) Savarkar were imprisoned in the Cellular Jail, unknowingly held in the same building for a time.

questions.q: How many of the jail's original seven wings survive today as part of the memorial?
questions.explanation: Several wings were demolished over time, and three of the original seven wings remain standing at the memorial today.

questions.q: Who was the much-feared jailer notorious for his brutality at the Cellular Jail in its early years?
questions.explanation: David Barry was the jailer infamous among prisoners for the severe and cruel discipline he enforced at the Cellular Jail.

questions.q: Indian prisoners convicted in which 1908 Bengal bomb case were among early inmates of the Cellular Jail?
questions.explanation: Revolutionaries convicted in the 1908 Alipore Bomb Case, such as Barindra Kumar Ghosh, were transported to the Cellular Jail.

questions.q: Which young Punjabi revolutionary died after a hunger strike at the Cellular Jail in 1933, becoming a martyr there?
questions.explanation: Mahavir Singh, a comrade of Bhagat Singh, died during the 1933 hunger strike when force-feeding caused fatal injury.

questions.q: During the Second World War, which power briefly occupied the Andaman Islands and the Cellular Jail?
questions.explanation: Japanese forces occupied the Andaman Islands from 1942 to 1945, taking control of the Cellular Jail during their occupation.

questions.q: The daily oil-grinding quota imposed on prisoners at the kolhu was notoriously set at roughly how much?
questions.explanation: Prisoners were expected to produce around thirty pounds of oil a day by hand on the kolhu, an almost impossible burden that brought punishment when unmet.

questions.q: After his Andaman visit, what did the Indian government later do to commemorate the jail's significance?
options: Sold it to a private trust
Declared it a National Memorial in 1979
questions.explanation: The Cellular Jail was dedicated to the nation as a National Memorial in 1979 to honour the freedom fighters held there.

questions.q: What practice did the British use to staff the Andaman settlement's labour besides political prisoners?
options: Transporting ordinary convicts as penal labour
questions.explanation: The Andaman penal settlement also relied on ordinary convicts transported from the mainland as forced penal labour to develop the islands.

questions.q: Which gallows feature, still preserved at the jail, was used to execute prisoners?
questions.explanation: The Cellular Jail preserves its gallows, built to hang multiple prisoners at once, as a grim reminder of the executions carried out there.
